chain_name: MLF → malic-to-lactic conversion → softer acid profile

starting_factor.description: Malolactic fermentation (MLF) is initiated by lactic acid bacteria, primarily Oenococcus oeni, after primary alcoholic fermentation.

intermediate_steps.description: Oenococcus oeni bacteria metabolise malic acid (a diprotic, sharp-tasting organic acid) and convert it to lactic acid (a monoprotic, softer-tasting organic acid) plus CO₂.

Because lactic acid is monoprotic (one dissociable proton per molecule) versus malic acid's two protons, the same molar quantity of lactic acid releases fewer hydrogen ions. Total titratable acidity decreases; pH rises slightly.
Tartaric acid is chemically stable and completely unaffected by MLF. Any tartaric-dominated wine will retain significant acidity even after full MLF.

final_outcome.description: The wine's acid profile shifts from sharp/angular (malic-dominated) to softer/rounder (lactic-dominated). Total acidity decreases slightly, pH rises slightly (typically 0.1–0.2 units). Tartaric acid fraction is unchanged.

examples.description: Full MLF converts sharp green-apple malic notes to creamy lactic character. The resulting acidity is softer and integrates better with oak and secondary fermentation aromas.

examples.region_or_wine: Muscadet Sèvre et Maine sur Lie
examples.description: Partial or no MLF (often blocked in Muscadet) preserves high malic acidity and a zippy, saline freshness that would be lost if full MLF occurred.

related_exam_questions: Explain what happens to acidity during malolactic fermentation.
Why might a winemaker choose to block MLF in a white wine?
What is the difference between malic acid and lactic acid in terms of sensory perception?
distinction_note: A Distinction answer explains the diprotic vs. monoprotic chemistry, distinguishes malic and tartaric acid behaviour (tartaric is MLF-stable), and links the winemaker's decision to block or allow MLF to deliberate stylistic intent — not just as something that 'happens'.
